title: Dog Laceration on Ear Flap: First Aid and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out what to do when your dog's ear flap is bleeding. Learn about first aid, urgency levels, and why triage photos are essential for care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ear flap lacerations cause significant bleeding and require prompt veterinary care to manage the wound and prevent complications like infection or hematomas.

What exactly is a dog ear flap laceration?

An ear flap laceration is a cut, tear, or puncture to the pinna, which is the outer part of your dog's ear. This area is heavily supplied with blood vessels, which is why even a relatively small wound can result in significant, steady bleeding. When a dog shakes its head due to the discomfort or the sensation of blood, it often causes the wound to reopen or splatter, making the situation look quite dramatic.

Is a dog's ear flap laceration considered a veterinary emergency?

  • This condition is generally classified as a Medium Urgency situation.
  • While a lacerated ear is rarely life-threatening, the bleeding can be difficult to manage at home and the wound is highly susceptible to infection or the formation of an aural hematoma.
  • You should seek veterinary care promptly if the bleeding does not stop with pressure, if the cut is deep, or if it extends through the cartilage of the ear.

How does providing a photo help triage my dog's ear injury?

  • Providing a clear photo of the injury to a veterinary professional can significantly speed up the triage process.
  • A photo allows a vet to assess the severity, location, and type of wound without immediate physical contact, which can be stressful for a dog in pain.
  • It helps the clinical team determine if your dog needs immediate surgical repair (stitches) or if the wound can be managed with conservative wound care and bandaging.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

For a dog ear flap laceration, initial assessment includes wound location, hemorrhage control, and evaluation for underlying structural damage. Preparation involves applying sterile lubricant, clipping hair, sterile skin preparation, and wound lavage. Suturing large ear lacerations is often ineffective and not recommended; severe damage may require significant debridement or partial surgical removal. Rapid hematoma development is common; hematomas should be lanced. If primary closure is not indicated due to gross contamination, the wound may be closed with drains or treated as an open wound.

Chapter: Surgery, Emergency, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2009)

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding right now?
faqs.answer: Apply firm, continuous pressure to the site of the bleeding using a clean towel or gauze for at least 10 minutes without stopping to check if it has stopped.

faqs.question: Should I let my dog shake their head?
faqs.answer: No. Head shaking will prevent clotting and cause the wound to bleed more. Try to keep your dog calm or gently wrap the ear against the head using a soft bandage until you reach a vet.

faqs.question: Does my dog need stitches for an ear cut?
faqs.answer: Many ear lacerations do require stitches because the skin on the ear is thin and the constant movement of the ear flap makes it difficult for the wound to stay closed on its own.
